For the purpose of survey calculations the reactor core was considered as a bare right-circular cylinder 54 in. in diameter and 66 in. high.

The calculated neutron lifetime was 290 $\mu$ sec; the perturbation-theory estimate of the reactivity loss was $3.9 \times 10^{-4} \delta k / k$ , compared to $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ as obtained from calculation of both the perturbed and unperturbed cases.

Assuming that the only effect of a temperature increase is the removal of fuel from the core, the estimated temperature coefficient of reactivity was $-2.4 \times 10^{-5} (\delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}) / {}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ based on a volumetric expansion coefficient of the fuel salt of $1.0 \times 10^{-4} / {}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ and a $(\delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}) / (\delta \mathrm{M} / \mathrm{M})$ value of $0.24$ as obtained from the GNU calculations.

The final hot-leg insert was removed from INOR-8 forced-convection loop 9354-4 after 15,140 hr of operation.

As previously discussed, three inserts were installed at the end of the hot-leg section of this loop to provide data on the weight changes of INOR-8 in contact with a beryllium-base fluoride fuel mixture.
The operating schedule specified removal of one insert after 5000 hr of exposure, another after 10,000, and the third after 15,000.
Operation of the loop began in July 1958 under the conditions:
Salt mixture LiF-BeF2UF4 (62-37-1 mole %)
Examination of the third insert showed an average weight loss of $1.7 \, \text{mg/cm}^2$ , $\pm 6\%$ , along its 4-in. length.
If uniform removal of surface metal is assumed, this weight loss corresponds to a loss in wall thickness of 0.08 mil, $\pm 6\%$ .
The first two inserts, which were removed from the loop after 5000 and 10,000 hr, showed respective weight losses of $1.8\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , $\pm 2\%$ , and $2.1\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , $\pm 3\%$ .
The corresponding losses in wall thickness were calculated to be 0.08 and 0.09 mil, respectively.
The similarity of these values indicates that no significant weight loss occurred after the first 5000 hr of operation.
As previously reported, $^{2}$ Metallography of the 5000- and 10,000-hr inserts revealed no evidence of attack other than the formation of a thin corrosion film on the surface exposed to the salts.
A zone comprised of unusually small grains was also noted below the corrosion film on both inserts to a depth of 1 to 2 mils.
Metallography of a transverse section of the 15,000-hr insert revealed light surface roughening, as shown in Fig.
As in the case of the 5000- and 10,000-hr inserts, the thin corrosion film and the band of fine-grained material were again found.
The corrosion film was the same thickness as found on the 10,000-hr insert; however, the band of fine-grained material had increased in size, compared to the 10,000-hr insert.

The main heat exchanger of the MSRE will consist of approximately 250 INOR-8 (1/2 in. in OD, and 0.045 in. in wall thickness) tubes joined to a 1-1/2-in.-thick INOR-8 header.
The basic techniques are those previously used for fabricating heat exchange components containing thick tube sheets.9
Figure 4.6 is a cross-sectional view of the tube-to-header joint configuration selected for the heat exchanger.
Those conditions which give maximum weld penetration of 1.5t also cause severe "roll over" of the weld and thus a constriction in the tube entrance.
Brazing was conducted in dry hydrogen, using the 82 Au-18 Ni (wt %) alloy.

The brazing temperature was $1830^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and a rate-of-temperature rise of $3000^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ was selected, since it approximates that to be readily obtained with commercial facilities.
A large assembly containing about 20 tubes is being planned to further demonstrate reliability in making a large number of joints.
Most of the mechanical-property tests on INOR-8 were completed in 1959, and the results of this program are presented in a topical report.
It was decided that up to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the design stresses would be based on two-thirds of the $0.2\%$ offset yield strength, adjusted for the minimum specified yield strength.
This value was chosen to be 35,000 psi.
Above $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the design stresses are based on the stress to produce $1\%$ creep strain in $10^{5}$ hr.
Stresses in the creep range were obtained from Larsen-Miller plots of data at 1100, 1200, 1250, 1300, 1400, 1500, 1650, 1700, and $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Over 100 creep tests were performed,
with times ranging from 0.1 to 25,000 hr; the majority were performed between 1100 and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Extrapolation of the rupture stresses to $10^{5}$ hr revealed that they were well above the creep limits and did not affect the design stresses.
The allowable stresses are shown in Table 4.5.
A series of tensile tests at 1100 and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ were performed to determine the effect of creep strains of less than $2\%$ on the strength and ductility of INOR-8.
A general pattern of the results is presented in Table 4.6.
The only deleterious effect is on the elongation which, in our case, was a loss of $50\%$ after approximately 12,000 hr of exposure.

For design purposes, the MSRE fuel has been chosen at the composition $\mathsf{Li}^{7}\mathsf{F}-\mathsf{BeF}_2$ - $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ - $\mathsf{UF}_4$ (65-30-4-1 mole %; m. p. $450^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ ).

Chemically, this composition can be roughly approximated as $\mathsf{Li}_2$ - $\mathsf{BeF}_4$ to which 5 mole % of quadrivalent fluorides are added; however, the exact proportions are based on carefully established phase diagrams showing the primary phase fields and lowest liquidus temperatures associated with a given $\mathsf{UF}_4$ and $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ content.
Furthermore, the $\mathsf{LiF}-\mathsf{BeF}_2$ solvent can hold as much as 15 mole % quadrivalent fluorides in solution without requiring temperatures in excess of $500^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

The preferred fuel compositions for MSRE-type reactors occur just outside the primary phase field of LiF, and thus take advantage of both the unusually large freezing-point depression of LiF caused by strongly "acidic" cations such as $\mathsf{Be}^{4+}$ , $\mathsf{Th}^{4+}$ , and $\mathsf{U}^{4+}$ and the diminished stability ranges of $\mathsf{3LiF}\cdot\mathsf{MF}_4$ crystals (as compared with 3:1 combinations containing other alkali fluorides).

The formulas and crystal structures of the intermediate compounds in this system were reported originally by Zachariasen.2 The cubic phases, $5\mathrm{NaF} \cdot 3\mathrm{UF}_4$ and $3\mathrm{NaF} \cdot 2\mathrm{ThF}_4$ , are structural analogs of a phase which Zachariasen identified as $\alpha$ -2KF $\cdot$ ThF; attempts to isolate and identify this phase are continuing.

Demonstration of a homogeneous solid phase at 75 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ThF_4}$ corroborates the view of Asker, Segnit and Wylie's that the compound of highest $\mathsf{ThF_4 / KF}$ ratio in the system is $\mathsf{KF} \cdot 3\mathsf{ThF}_4$ rather than $\mathsf{KF} \cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ , as was previously claimed.2 In fact, it now appears that $\mathsf{KF} \cdot 3\mathsf{ThF}_4$ melts incongruity to $\mathsf{ThF_4}$ ss and liquid, that it undergoes solid state transitions which account for the existence of three crystal polymorphs, and that it is unstable with respect to $\mathsf{KF} \cdot 2\mathsf{ThF}_4$ and $\mathsf{ThF_4}$ below $775^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

Although relatively detailed work had been made on the phases in the system KF-ThF4 before the current studies, the system RbF-ThF4 had been investigated in only a cursory fashion.3 Results of thermal gradient quenching experiments in the system RbF-ThF4 now reveal five intermediate compounds, 3RbF·ThF4, 2RbF·ThF4, 7RbF·6ThF4, RbF·3ThF4, and RbF·6ThF4, in contrast with 3RbF·ThF4, RbF·ThF4, and RbF·3ThF4 as reported by Dergunov and Bergman.

In conjunction with problems arising from the presence of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ in $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ -containing fuels, thermal gradient quenching experiments of binary $\mathrm{ZrF_4 - ThF_4}$ mixtures have shown that the system $\mathrm{ZrF_4 - ThF_4}$ forms continuous solid solutions with a minimum at approximately 23 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ and at $858^{\circ}$ .

5.3) differs substantially from that reported by Dergunov. $^{10}$ Results of ORNL phase studies have shown that the system contains the single binary compound LiF-YF $_3$ , melting incongruently to YF $_3$ and liquid at $815^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The peritectic invariant point for this reaction occurs at 49 mole $\%$ YF $_3$ .
A single eutectic occurs in the system LiF-YF $_3$ at 19 mole $\%$ YF $_3$ and at $695^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The structure of the compound LiF-YF $_3$ , determined from single crystal x-ray diffraction measurements, has been shown to be tetragonal. $^{11}$ Optical properties of the compound have been established.
In conjunction with the phase studies, the melting point of $\mathrm{YF}_3$ has been determined to be $(1144 \pm 3)^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A large exothermic effect in the $\mathrm{YF}_3$ heating and cooling curves indicates that $\mathrm{YF}_3$ undergoes a solid state transition at $1060^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The temperature of this transition has been confirmed by the results of thermal gradient quenching experiments, but it has not been possible to preserve the high-temperature modification of $\mathrm{YF}_3$ by quenching from temperatures above $1060^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Apparently $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ melts somewhere between 1400 and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , probably about 1425 or $1450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but attempts to measure the melting point have been thwarted by the volatilization of $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ at the requisite temperatures and by some poorly understood phenomena.
Previous reported values for the melting point, usually estimated,13-15 have ranged from 1027 to $1450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The growth of single crystals of $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ by zone melting is alleged to have occurred with a hot-zone temperature of $14200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Temperatures are measured with a Pt, Pt-10% Rh thermocouple located in a tantalum thermocouple well.
Sealed platinum capsules of 5-mil wall thickness ruptured from internal pressure developed by $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ at temperatures of 1400 to $1550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

(The extrapolated vapor pressure of solid $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ is $650\mathrm{mm}$ at $1450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .) In one experiment, it was observed that the rupturing of the capsule was accompanied by a flash of light.

Quantitative evaluations are not yet available because of unexplained discrepancies in some of the analytical results, but there are plausible indications that the inclusion of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ in the MSRE fuel, at a concentration of about 5 mole of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ for 1 mole $\mathrm{UF_4}$ , results in the solubility product for $\mathrm{ZrO_2}$ being exceeded at an oxide concentration too low to precipitate $\mathrm{UO_2}$ .
A black precipitate of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ settles to the bottom until the end point is reached, when the green color of $\mathrm{UF}_4$ (detectible at 300 ppm) becomes apparent.

The ionic radius of $\mathrm{U}^{+4}$ (0.97A) is sufficiently close to that of $\mathrm{Zr}^{+4}$ (0.80A) that a solid-solid solution was considered likely.
Reaction of $\mathrm{ZrF}_4$ with $\mathrm{UO}_2$ at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ yielded $\mathrm{UF}_4$ and $\mathrm{ZrO}_2$ rather than the hypothetical $\mathrm{UZr(OF}_2)_2$ .
